Found across temple balustrades, lintels, pediments, and ceremonial stairways, the Naga is not merely decorative—it is a guardian of sacred space, a protector of divine order, and a symbol of royal legitimacy and cosmic balance.  ...    In Indian mythology, the Nāga (Sanskrit: "serpent") is a semi-divine being associated with water, fertility, protection, and the underworld. Often depicted as multi-headed cobras, Nagas inhabit rivers, oceans, and subterranean  ...    This symbolism was transmitted to mainland Southeast Asia through Hinduism and Buddhism, where it was quickly integrated with indigenous serpent cults already prevalent among local animistic traditions. The love story between Kaundinya and Soma is the foundation for many standard practices in modern-day Khmer culture, including wedding ceremonies and other rituals. The Khmer people regard themselves as descendants of the nāgas and many still believe the nāga exist today, destined to one day return and restore prosperity to their people.  In Khmer culture, nāgas symbolize rain, and represent a bridge between the mortal realm (ឋានមនុស្ស) and the realm of devas(Heaven; ឋានទេវតា/ឋានសួគ៌).
